title: Genomic Epidemiology of Influenza A: Tracking Viral Spread from Avian Flyways to Human Communities
keywords: phylogeographic transmission inference with bayes factors

summary.hook: This poster uses genomic surveillance to trace influenza A spread from avian flyways into human communities, with a focus on evidence that a Central flyway can act as a secondary hub after introductions from the Pacific.
summary.summary_30s: This poster uses genomic surveillance to trace influenza A spread from avian flyways into human communities, with a focus on evidence that a Central flyway can act as a secondary hub after introductions from the Pacific. It highlights a rapid campus expansion of an influenza subclade K following a spike in student health diagnoses in November 2025, with evidence consistent with multiple introductions and onward campus-to-community transmission. Quantitative highlights include a mapped “Decisive Bayes factor support” of 20,243 for pairwise transmission, an estimated median evolutionary rate of 7.93×10⁻³ substitutions/site/year, and an inferred cryptic circulation period of 6–16 months for H5N1 D1.1.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Track influenza A viral spread using genomic epidemiology from avian flyways into human communities, including campus-to-community dynamics.

Approach: Population: Wild birds (species examples listed) and human cases (student health diagnoses). Primary outcome: Evidence for transmission pathways (flyways and community), evolutionary rate estimates, and clustering patterns.
Finding: Flyways & spread: The Central flyway is described as a secondary hub, initially a sink for introductions from the Pacific, then amplifying viral diversity and driving eastward spread.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Genomic surveillance can reveal hidden influenza spread pathways across flyways and into human communities; Central flyway can serve as a hub; campus subclade expansion shows multiple introductions and onward spread; antigenic drift and rapid evolution underscore challenges for control. Implications (poster-grounded): Surveillance should integrate wild-bird flyway dynamics with human-community monitoring to detect cryptic circulation and spillover pathways. Campus residence structure can shape transmission dynamics and may be an important target for public health interventions. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Not stated on poster (mechanistic transmission pathways are inferred via genomics and flyway movement). Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): * Reference list includes a 2026 Euro Surveillance manuscript on Subclade K and an under-review manuscript on HPAI H5 clade 2.3.4.4b genotype D1.1 across North American flyways (fall 2024 migration).
key_numbers: Timeline shown spanning Nov–Dec 2025; cryptic circulation inferred 6–16 months (for H5N1 D1.1).
Decisive Bayes factor support for pairwise flyway transmission shown as 20,243 for Pacific→Central and 20,243 for Central→Mississippi.
Median evolutionary rate for H5 HA estimated at 7.93×10⁻³ substitutions/site/year.
H5N1 D1.1 likely circulated undetected for 6–16 months (cryptic transmission).
Antigenic drift: 10 subclade K HA substitutions in RBD/antigenic sites; epitope modeling predicts low VE vs vaccine strain (0.13).
Arizona surveillance: 6.5% H5 avian flu positivity in wild birds.
takeaways: Flyways & spread: The Central flyway is described as a secondary hub, initially a sink for introductions from the Pacific, then amplifying viral diversity and driving eastward spread.
Transmission support: “Decisive Bayes factor support for pairwise transmission” is shown, with values 20,243 for Pacific→Central and 20,243 for Central→Mississippi.

Campus subclade expansion: “Rapid expansion of Subclade K on campus” with a spike in influenza diagnoses at Student Health in November 2025 (date callout 8 November 2025). Spread described as multiple introductions with onward campus-to-community spread (phylodynamics).
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What specific influenza A lineage(s) are analyzed in each section (H5N1 D1.1 vs subclade K), and how are they related?

what_to_ask.question: The poster frames the Pacific flyway as the primary source for D1.1 expansion—what is the evidence supporting that directionality?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How sensitive are the Bayes factor transmission results to sampling bias across flyways (uneven sequence density)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What are the best actionable surveillance interventions suggested by your “hidden pathways” framing?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
